title: Find all duplicates
description: Find all elements appearing twice in array of size n

examples.explanation: Array: [4,3,2,7,8,2,3,1], n=8. Initialize result = []. Process i=0 (element=4): count=0, j=0 to 7: compare 4 with each element → count=1 (only at index 0). count≠2, skip. i=1 (element=3): count=0, j=0 to 7: compare 3 with elements → matches at indices 1 and 6, count=2. Check if 3 already in result → no, add 3 to result = [3]. i=2 (element=2): count=0, j=0 to 7: compare 2 with elements → matches at indices 2 and 5, count=2. Check if 2 already in result → no, add 2 to result = [3,2]. i=3 (element=7): count=0, j=0 to 7: compare 7 → count=1, skip. i=4 (element=8): count=0, j=0 to 7: compare 8 → count=1, skip. i=5 (element=2): count=0, but 2 already in result, skip counting (or count and skip adding). i=6 (element=3): already in result, skip. i=7 (element=1): count=0, j=0 to 7: compare 1 → count=1, skip. Final result = [3,2] (order may vary, typically insertion order).

Array: [1,1,1,2,2,3], n=6. Initialize result = []. i=0 (element=1): count=0, j=0 to 5: compare 1 → matches at indices 0,1,2, count=3. count≠2, skip. i=1 (element=1): already processed (can skip if tracking visited). i=2 (element=1): skip. i=3 (element=2): count=0, j=0 to 5: compare 2 → matches at indices 3 and 4, count=2. Check result → not present, add 2 to result = [2]. i=4 (element=2): already in result, skip. i=5 (element=3): count=0, j=0 to 5: compare 3 → count=1, skip. Final result = [2].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Compare each element with every other element in the array to find duplicates. Use nested loops to check all pairs and collect elements that appear exactly twice.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Duplicate Finder
ladders.desc: Complete solution using nested loops to find all elements appearing exactly twice in an array.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to find all elements that appear exactly twice in an array. The intuition is straightforward: we need to check every element against every other element to count occurrences. Since we're looking for elements that appear exactly twice, we need to ensure we don't count the element against itself and we avoid double-counting duplicates we've already found. The algorithm uses two nested loops: the outer loop picks each element as a candidate, and the inner loop counts how many times that candidate appears in the entire array. We only add an element to our result if its count equals 2 and we haven't already recorded it. The time complexity is O(n²) because for each of n elements, we potentially scan all n elements. Space complexity is O(1) excluding the output storage, as we only use a few variables for counting and tracking. This connects directly to L1's key subroutine of counting occurrences for a single element. Common mistakes include counting the element against itself (which would give false positives), not checking if we've already recorded a duplicate (leading to duplicate results), and misunderstanding 'exactly twice' versus 'at least twice'. The brute force approach is conceptually simple but inefficient for large arrays, making it a good starting point before learning optimized approaches.

examples.trace: Array: [4,3,2,7,8,2,3,1], n=8. Initialize result = []. Process i=0 (element=4): count=0, j=0 to 7: compare 4 with each element → count=1 (only at index 0). count≠2, skip. i=1 (element=3): count=0, j=0 to 7: compare 3 with elements → matches at indices 1 and 6, count=2. Check if 3 already in result → no, add 3 to result = [3]. i=2 (element=2): count=0, j=0 to 7: compare 2 with elements → matches at indices 2 and 5, count=2. Check if 2 already in result → no, add 2 to result = [3,2]. i=3 (element=7): count=0, j=0 to 7: compare 7 → count=1, skip. i=4 (element=8): count=0, j=0 to 7: compare 8 → count=1, skip. i=5 (element=2): count=0, but 2 already in result, skip counting (or count and skip adding). i=6 (element=3): already in result, skip. i=7 (element=1): count=0, j=0 to 7: compare 1 → count=1, skip. Final result = [3,2] (order may vary, typically insertion order).

Array: [1,1,1,2,2,3], n=6. Initialize result = []. i=0 (element=1): count=0, j=0 to 5: compare 1 → matches at indices 0,1,2, count=3. count≠2, skip. i=1 (element=1): already processed (can skip if tracking visited). i=2 (element=1): skip. i=3 (element=2): count=0, j=0 to 5: compare 2 → matches at indices 3 and 4, count=2. Check result → not present, add 2 to result = [2]. i=4 (element=2): already in result, skip. i=5 (element=3): count=0, j=0 to 5: compare 3 → count=1, skip. Final result = [2].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Occurrences of a Single Element
ladders.desc: Count how many times a specific target element appears in an array using linear scan.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach: counting occurrences of a given element in an array. The intuition is that to determine if an element appears exactly twice, we first need to know how many times it appears. We scan through the entire array, comparing each element to the target, and increment a counter whenever we find a match. This is a linear search adapted for counting. The algorithm is simple: initialize count = 0, loop through each element in the array, if the current element equals the target, increment count. After the loop, count holds the total occurrences. Time complexity is O(n) for an array of size n, as we examine each element once. Space complexity is O(1), using only the count variable. This feeds into L0 by being called for each element in the outer loop, making the overall complexity O(n²). Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds, forgetting to reset count for each new target, and incorrectly handling edge cases like empty arrays. This sub-routine is fundamental in many algorithms beyond duplicate finding, such as frequency analysis.

examples.trace: Array = [4,3,2,7,8,2,3,1], target=2, count=0. Index 0: 4 ≠ 2, count=0. Index 1: 3 ≠ 2, count=0. Index 2: 2 == 2, count=1. Index 3: 7 ≠ 2, count=1. Index 4: 8 ≠ 2, count=1. Index 5: 2 == 2, count=2. Index 6: 3 ≠ 2, count=2. Index 7: 1 ≠ 2, count=2. Final count = 2.

Array = [1,1,1,2,2,3], target=1, count=0. Index 0: 1 == 1, count=1. Index 1: 1 == 1, count=2. Index 2: 1 == 1, count=3. Index 3: 2 ≠ 1, count=3. Index 4: 2 ≠ 1, count=3. Index 5: 3 ≠ 1, count=3. Final count = 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Nested Loop Comparison Pattern
ladders.desc: Use two nested loops to compare each element with every other element in the array.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the nested loop pattern that enables pairwise comparisons in an array. The intuition is that to find relationships between elements (like duplicates), we need to examine all possible pairs (i, j) where i and j are indices. The outer loop iterates through each element as a reference, and the inner loop iterates through all elements to compare against the reference. This creates n * n comparisons in the worst case, giving O(n²) time. The key insight is that this pattern allows us to check every element against every other, which is necessary when we have no additional information about the array. In the context of finding duplicates, we use this to count occurrences for each element. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use loop indices and temporary variables. This feeds into L1 by providing the structure to repeatedly call the counting sub-routine. Common mistakes include using the same index for both loops (leading to self-comparison), incorrect loop bounds causing missed comparisons or out-of-bounds errors, and not optimizing to avoid redundant comparisons (though optimization isn't needed for the basic brute force). This pattern is foundational for many brute force algorithms in DSA.

examples.trace: Array = [4,3,2], n=3. Outer loop i from 0 to 2: i=0, element=4. Inner loop j from 0 to 2: j=0: compare 4 with 4 (self, often skipped in duplicate check). j=1: compare 4 with 3. j=2: compare 4 with 2. i=1, element=3. j=0: compare 3 with 4. j=1: compare 3 with 3 (self). j=2: compare 3 with 2. i=2, element=2. j=0: compare 2 with 4. j=1: compare 2 with 3. j=2: compare 2 with 2 (self). Total comparisons: 3*3=9, but self-comparisons may be excluded based on implementation.

examples.output: All comparisons: (1,1) (if self allowed) or just cross-comparison
examples.trace: Array = [1,1], n=2. i=0, element=1. j=0: compare 1 with 1 (self). j=1: compare 1 with 1 (duplicate check). i=1, element=1. j=0: compare 1 with 1. j=1: compare 1 with 1 (self). For duplicate finding, we might skip j when i==j to avoid counting self.

testCases.input: Array: [1, 2, 3], list all i,j pairs (i=outer, j=inner)
Array: [1, 1, 1], count comparisons if skipping i==j
Array: [0, 0, 0, 0, 0], comparisons for i=2, j all
Array: [9, 8, 7], list pairs where i<j (upper triangle)

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Index Tracking
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array using indices to access and compare elements.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing an array using indices, which is essential for the nested loop pattern. The intuition is that arrays are indexed collections, and to perform operations like comparisons, we need to access each element via its position. We use a loop (e.g., for or while) with an index variable that goes from 0 to n-1, where n is the array length. At each step, we access array[index] to get the current element. This allows us to examine or modify elements in sequence. In the context of duplicate finding, we use this for both the outer and inner loops to navigate the array. Time complexity for a single traversal is O(n), and space is O(1) for the index variable. This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to implement the loops for pairwise comparisons. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using <= n instead of < n), confusing zero-based indexing, and not handling empty arrays (where n=0). This skill is fundamental to almost all array algorithms and is a prerequisite for more complex data structure manipulations.

examples.input: Array: [10, 20, 30], traverse and print each element
examples.trace: Array = [10,20,30], n=3. Initialize index i=0. i=0: access array[0]=10, print 10. Increment i to 1. i=1: access array[1]=20, print 20. Increment i to 2. i=2: access array[2]=30, print 30. Increment i to 3, which is >= n, stop.

examples.input: Array: [5, 5, 5], traverse and count elements equal to 5
examples.trace: Array = [5,5,5], n=3. Initialize count=0, i=0. i=0: array[0]=5, equals 5, count=1. i=1: array[1]=5, count=2. i=2: array[2]=5, count=3. i=3, stop. Final count=3.

testCases.input: Array: [9, 8, 7, 6], print in reverse via indices
Array: [3, 3, 3], check if all elements equal 3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Comparison in Arrays
ladders.desc: Compare two elements in an array to check if they are equal.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two elements from an array to determine if they have the same value. The intuition is simple: in many algorithms, including duplicate finding, we need to identify when two items match. We access two elements using their indices (e.g., array[i] and array[j]) and use a comparison operator (== in most languages) to check equality. If they are equal, we might increment a counter or take some other action. This operation is constant time, O(1), as it involves a single comparison. Space complexity is O(1), using no extra memory beyond the elements being compared. This feeds into L3 by being the core action performed during array traversal when looking for duplicates. Common mistakes include comparing indices instead of elements (e.g., i == j instead of array[i] == array[j]), not considering data types (e.g., integer vs string comparison), and misunderstanding equality versus identity in some languages. This operation is ubiquitous in programming and is foundational for sorting, searching, and many other algorithms.

examples.input: Array: [4, 3, 2], compare element at index 0 and index 2
examples.trace: Array = [4,3,2]. Access array[0] = 4. Access array[2] = 2. Compare 4 == 2: false.

examples.input: Array: [1, 1, 2], compare element at index 0 and index 1
examples.trace: Array = [1,1,2]. Access array[0] = 1. Access array[1] = 1. Compare 1 == 1: true.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand what an array is and how to access elements using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces arrays, which are fundamental data structures in programming. An array is a collection of elements, each identified by an index. Intuitively, think of it like a row of boxes numbered starting from 0: you can store a value in each box and retrieve it by its number. The key concepts are: arrays have a fixed size (n), elements are of the same type, and indexing starts at 0 (so the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, up to index n-1). To access an element, use the syntax array[index], e.g., if array = [10, 20, 30], then array[0] gives 10. This is constant time, O(1), because the computer can directly calculate the memory location. Space complexity for the array itself is O(n) to store n elements. This feeds into L4 by providing the structure on which comparisons are performed. Common mistakes include using index 1 for the first element (off-by-one), accessing indices outside 0 to n-1 (causing errors), and confusing arrays with lists in some languages. Understanding arrays is essential for nearly all algorithms, as they are the simplest way to store and process sequential data.

examples.trace: Array = [5,10,15]. Indices: 0→5, 1→10, 2→15. To access index 1, look at the element at position 1: it is 10.
Array = [100,200]. The elements are in order: at index 0 is 100, at index 1 is 200. So 200 is at index 1.

testCases.input: Array: [100], what is the last index?
Array: [7, 14, 21], what value is at index 0?

approaches.name: HashMap Frequency Count O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a hash map to count the frequency of each element in one pass through the array. Then, iterate through the map to collect elements with frequency exactly 2.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Duplicates Using HashMap Frequency Count
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find all elements appearing exactly twice in an array using hash map frequency counting.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires identifying elements that appear exactly twice in an array of size n. The HashMap Frequency Count approach works by leveraging a hash map's O(1) average time complexity for insertions and lookups. The intuition is simple: we need to track how many times each element appears, and elements with a count of 2 are our duplicates. We iterate through the array once, using each element as a key in the hash map and incrementing its count. After building the frequency map, we iterate through the map entries to collect keys with value exactly 2. This approach handles any integer values (including negative numbers) and works regardless of array order. The time complexity is O(n) because we make one pass through the array and one pass through the map (which has at most n entries). Space complexity is O(n) for storing the frequency map. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling edge cases like empty arrays or arrays with no duplicates, 2) Using arrays instead of hash maps for potentially large or sparse value ranges, 3) Forgetting to initialize counts properly, 4) Misunderstanding that we need elements appearing exactly twice (not at least twice). The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the frequency counting subroutine as its core component. Real code pattern: def findDuplicates(nums): freq = {}; for num in nums: freq[num] = freq.get(num, 0) + 1; result = []; for num, count in freq.items(): if count == 2: result.append(num); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty hash map freq = {}. Step 2: Iterate through array: Process 4 → freq = {4:1}. Process 3 → freq = {4:1, 3:1}. Process 2 → freq = {4:1, 3:1, 2:1}. Process 7 → freq = {4:1, 3:1, 2:1, 7:1}. Process 8 → freq = {4:1, 3:1, 2:1, 7:1, 8:1}. Process 2 → freq[2] currently 1, increment to 2 → freq = {4:1, 3:1, 2:2, 7:1, 8:1}. Process 3 → freq[3] currently 1, increment to 2 → freq = {4:1, 3:2, 2:2, 7:1, 8:1}. Process 1 → freq = {4:1, 3:2, 2:2, 7:1, 8:1, 1:1}. Step 3: Iterate through freq map: Check 4: count=1 (not 2). Check 3: count=2 → add 3 to result. Check 2: count=2 → add 2 to result. Check 7: count=1. Check 8: count=1. Check 1: count=1. Result = [3, 2] (order may vary, typically sorted or insertion order).

Step 1: Initialize freq = {}. Step 2: Iterate: Process 1 → freq={1:1}. Process 1 → freq[1]=1 → increment to 2 → freq={1:2}. Process 2 → freq={1:2, 2:1}. Process 2 → freq[2]=1 → increment to 2 → freq={1:2, 2:2}. Process 3 → freq={1:2, 2:2, 3:1}. Process 4 → freq={1:2, 2:2, 3:1, 4:1}. Process 5 → freq={1:2, 2:2, 3:1, 4:1, 5:1}. Step 3: Iterate through freq: Check 1: count=2 → add 1. Check 2: count=2 → add 2. Check 3: count=1. Check 4: count=1. Check 5: count=1. Result = [1, 2].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Element Frequencies in Array
ladders.desc: Build a frequency map counting occurrences of each element in an array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the full solution: creating a frequency map that counts how many times each element appears in an array. The intuition is that we need an efficient way to track counts for potentially many distinct values. A hash map (dictionary) provides O(1) average time for insertions and updates, making it ideal. We iterate through the array once. For each element, we check if it's already in the map: if yes, increment its count; if no, add it with count 1. This builds a complete frequency distribution. The time complexity is O(n) for the single pass through n elements. Space complexity is O(k) where k is the number of distinct elements (at most n). Common mistakes: 1) Using an array instead of hash map when value range is unknown or large, 2) Not handling the first occurrence correctly (need to initialize count to 1, not 0), 3) Using inefficient data structures that don't provide O(1) operations. This feeds into L0 by providing the frequency data needed to identify duplicates. Real code pattern: def countFrequencies(nums): freq = {}; for num in nums: if num in freq: freq[num] += 1; else: freq[num] = 1; return freq. Alternative: freq[num] = freq.get(num, 0) + 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize freq = {}. Step 2: Process 1 → not in freq → add 1:1 → freq={1:1}. Step 3: Process 2 → not in freq → add 2:1 → freq={1:1, 2:1}. Step 4: Process 1 → in freq (value 1) → increment to 2 → freq={1:2, 2:1}. Step 5: Process 3 → not in freq → add 3:1 → freq={1:2, 2:1, 3:1}. Step 6: Process 2 → in freq (value 1) → increment to 2 → freq={1:2, 2:2, 3:1}. Step 7: Process 1 → in freq (value 2) → increment to 3 → freq={1:3, 2:2, 3:1}.

Step 1: freq = {}. Step 2: Process 5 → not in freq → add 5:1 → freq={5:1}. Step 3: Process 5 → in freq (value 1) → increment to 2 → freq={5:2}. Step 4: Process 5 → in freq (value 2) → increment to 3 → freq={5:3}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identify Elements with Specific Frequency from Map
ladders.desc: Filter a frequency map to extract keys with a target count value.
ladders.explanation: This core logic demonstrates how to use a frequency map to solve problems: after counting frequencies, we often need to extract elements meeting specific criteria. Here, we filter for elements with count exactly 2. The intuition is that a frequency map transforms the problem from searching arrays to simple dictionary lookups. We iterate through the map's key-value pairs, checking if the value (frequency) equals our target (2). If yes, we add the key to our result list. This is efficient because map iteration is O(k) where k is number of distinct elements. The connection to L1 is that this logic uses the frequency map produced by the counting subroutine. Common mistakes: 1) Comparing with wrong operator (e.g., >= instead of ==), 2) Not handling cases where no elements meet the criteria, 3) Confusing keys and values. This technique generalizes to many frequency-based problems (e.g., find elements appearing once, more than n/2 times, etc.). Real code pattern: def filterByFrequency(freq_map, target): result = []; for key, value in freq_map.items(): if value == target: result.append(key); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result = []. Step 2: Iterate through map items: First item: key=1, value=3 → 3 != 2 → skip. Second item: key=2, value=2 → 2 == 2 → add 2 to result → result=[2]. Third item: key=3, value=1 → 1 != 2 → skip. Step 3: Return result = [2].

Step 1: result = []. Step 2: Iterate: key=4, value=1 → 1 != 2 → skip. key=5, value=2 → 2 == 2 → add 5 → result=[5]. key=6, value=2 → 2 == 2 → add 6 → result=[5,6]. key=7, value=1 → skip. Step 3: Return [5,6].

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate and Update Dictionary Values
ladders.desc: Process a collection while maintaining and updating counts in a dictionary.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches the fundamental pattern of updating dictionary values during iteration. The intuition is that dictionaries allow us to maintain state (like counts) keyed by some identifier. As we process each item in a collection, we need to either initialize its entry (if first seen) or update its existing value. This pattern appears in countless algorithms beyond frequency counting. The key operations are: 1) Check if key exists in dictionary, 2) If exists, update its value (e.g., increment), 3) If not exists, add key with initial value. Time complexity per operation is O(1) average for hash maps. Space grows with number of distinct keys. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to handle the 'key not found' case, 2) Using wrong update logic (e.g., setting instead of incrementing), 3) Not initializing properly. This feeds into L2 by providing the updated dictionary needed for filtering. Real code pattern: for item in collection: if item in dict: dict[item] += 1; else: dict[item] = 1. Or using get(): dict[item] = dict.get(item, 0) + 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with dict = {}. Step 2: Process 'a' → 'a' not in dict → add 'a':1 → dict={'a':1}. Step 3: Process 'b' → 'b' not in dict → add 'b':1 → dict={'a':1, 'b':1}. Step 4: Process 'a' → 'a' in dict (value 1) → increment to 2 → dict={'a':2, 'b':1}. Step 5: Process 'c' → 'c' not in dict → add 'c':1 → dict={'a':2, 'b':1, 'c':1}.

Step 1: dict = {}. Step 2: Process 5 → not in dict → add 5:1 → dict={5:1}. Step 3: Process 5 → in dict (value 1) → increment to 2 → dict={5:2}. Step 4: Process 5 → in dict (value 2) → increment to 3 → dict={5:3}. Step 5: Process 5 → in dict (value 3) → increment to 4 → dict={5:4}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Dictionary Lookup and Insertion
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of checking if a key exists and inserting/updating values in a dictionary.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the atomic operations that make hash maps useful: looking up whether a key exists, and inserting or updating its associated value. The intuition is that dictionaries provide fast access to data via keys, much like a real dictionary lets you find word definitions quickly. Lookup involves checking if a key is present (typically O(1) average time). Insertion adds a new key-value pair; updating modifies the value for an existing key. These operations are building blocks for maintaining state during algorithms. Common mistakes: 1) Assuming a key exists without checking (leading to KeyError), 2) Confusing keys and values, 3) Not understanding that dictionaries are unordered (though Python 3.7+ maintains insertion order). This feeds into L3 by providing the tools to implement the iterate-and-update pattern. Real code patterns: Check: if key in dict:. Insert: dict[key] = value. Update: dict[key] = new_value. Combined: dict[key] = dict.get(key, default) + increment.

examples.input: Dict: {'a':1, 'b':2}, operations: Check 'a' exists, update 'a' to 3, insert 'c' with 4
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with dict = {'a':1, 'b':2}. Step 2: Check if 'a' exists → yes (value 1). Step 3: Update 'a' to 3 → dict becomes {'a':3, 'b':2}. Step 4: Insert 'c' with value 4 → dict becomes {'a':3, 'b':2, 'c':4}.

examples.input: Dict: {}, operations: Check 'x' exists, insert 'x' with 5, update 'x' to 6
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with dict = {}. Step 2: Check if 'x' exists → no. Step 3: Insert 'x' with value 5 → dict = {'x':5}. Step 4: Update 'x' to 6 → dict = {'x':6}.

testCases.input: Dict: {'a':1, 'b':2}, operations: Check 'a' exists, update 'a' to 3, insert 'c' with 4
Dict: {}, operations: Check 'x' exists, insert 'x' with 5, update 'x' to 6
Dict: {1:10}, operations: Check 2 exists, insert 2 with 20
Dict: {5:1, 6:2}, operations: Check 5 exists, update 5 to 0
Dict: {'x':1}, operations: Check 'y' exists, insert 'y' with 2
Dict: {0:0}, operations: Update 0 to 1, insert 1 with 2
Dict: {10:100}, operations: Check 10 exists, update 10 to 200

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Key-Value Pairs in Dictionaries
ladders.desc: Basic introduction to dictionaries as collections of key-value pairs.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces dictionaries (hash maps), which are fundamental data structures that store data as key-value pairs. Think of a real dictionary: the word is the key, and the definition is the value. In programming, a dictionary allows you to quickly look up a value using its associated key. Keys must be unique (like words in a dictionary), while values can be duplicates. The intuition is that this structure provides fast access because keys are hashed to compute storage locations. Basic operations include creating a dictionary, adding pairs, accessing values by key, and checking if a key exists. Dictionaries are unordered in theory (though Python maintains insertion order), meaning you can't rely on positional indexing like with lists. This concept feeds into L4 by providing the fundamental understanding needed for lookup and insertion operations. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Trying to use mutable objects as keys (only immutable types like strings, numbers, tuples work), 2) Confusing dictionaries with lists, 3) Not understanding that keys are unique. Real code patterns: Create: d = {} or d = {'key1': value1, 'key2': value2}. Add: d[key] = value. Access: value = d[key]. Check: if key in d.

examples.input: Create dictionary with pairs: name->'Alice', age->30, city->'NYC'
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with empty dictionary: d = {}. Step 2: Add key 'name' with value 'Alice': d['name'] = 'Alice' → d = {'name':'Alice'}. Step 3: Add key 'age' with value 30: d['age'] = 30 → d = {'name':'Alice', 'age':30}. Step 4: Add key 'city' with value 'NYC': d['city'] = 'NYC' → d = {'name':'Alice', 'age':30, 'city':'NYC'}.

examples.input: Dictionary: {'fruit':'apple', 'color':'red'}, operations: Access 'fruit', check if 'size' exists
examples.trace: Step 1: Given dictionary d = {'fruit':'apple', 'color':'red'}. Step 2: Access value for key 'fruit': d['fruit'] returns 'apple'. Step 3: Check if key 'size' exists in d: 'size' in d returns False because 'size' is not a key in the dictionary.

testCases.input: Create dictionary with pairs: name->'Alice', age->30, city->'NYC'
Dictionary: {'fruit':'apple', 'color':'red'}, operations: Access 'fruit', check if 'size' exists
Dictionary: {'x':10, 'y':20}, operation: Add key 'z' with value 30
Create dictionary with single pair: id->100

approaches.name: Sort and Scan O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Sort the array first, then scan through it to identify consecutive duplicate elements. Check for exactly two occurrences by comparing adjacent elements.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) (if sorting in-place, excluding output storage)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Duplicates Using Sort and Scan
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find all elements appearing exactly twice in an array using sorting and linear scan.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by first sorting the array to bring duplicate elements together, then scanning through the sorted array to identify elements that appear exactly twice. The intuition is that after sorting, any duplicate values will be adjacent to each other, making them easy to detect with a single pass. Sorting transforms the problem from needing to track frequencies across the entire array (which would require extra space) to simply comparing neighbors. The algorithm begins by sorting the array in-place if possible (O(n log n) time). Then, we iterate through the sorted array, comparing each element with its next neighbor. When we find two consecutive equal elements, we check if they form exactly two occurrences by ensuring the element before and after are different (or we're at boundaries). We add such elements to the result list. The time complexity is dominated by sorting: O(n log n) for comparison-based sorts. Space complexity is O(1) excluding the output storage, as we sort in-place and use a few variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete duplicate detection logic after sorting. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle array boundaries properly when checking for exactly two occurrences, incorrectly counting more than two duplicates, or not sorting first. The real code pattern involves a sort followed by a while loop with careful index management.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort the array: [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 7, 8]. Step 2: Initialize i = 0, result = []. Step 3: While i < n (8): i=0, arr[0]=1, arr[1]=2, not equal, i++ to 1. i=1, arr[1]=2, arr[2]=2, equal, check if exactly two: (i==0 or arr[0]=1 !=2) and (i+2==3>=n or arr[3]=3 !=2), yes, add 2 to result, i+=2 to 3. i=3, arr[3]=3, arr[4]=3, equal, check: (i==0 or arr[2]=2 !=3) and (i+2=5<n and arr[5]=4 !=3), yes, add 3 to result, i+=2 to 5. i=5, arr[5]=4, arr[6]=7, not equal, i++ to 6. i=6, arr[6]=7, arr[7]=8, not equal, i++ to 7. i=7, i+1=8>=n, stop. Result: [2, 3].

Step 1: Sort: [1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3]. Step 2: i=0, result=[]. i=0, arr[0]=1, arr[1]=1, equal, check exactly two: (i==0) true for first part, but (i+2=2<n and arr[2]=1 ==1) so second part false (next element is also 1, meaning more than two), skip, i++ to 1. i=1, arr[1]=1, arr[2]=1, equal, check: (i>0 and arr[0]=1 ==1) first part false (prev is also 1), skip, i++ to 2. i=2, arr[2]=1, arr[3]=2, not equal, i++ to 3. i=3, arr[3]=2, arr[4]=2, equal, check: (i==0 or arr[2]=1 !=2) true and (i+2=5<n and arr[5]=3 !=2) true, add 2 to result, i+=2 to 5. i=5, i+1=6>=n, stop. Result: [2].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Detect Exactly Two Consecutive Duplicates in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Scan a sorted array to find elements that appear exactly twice consecutively.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core scanning logic after sorting. The intuition is that in a sorted array, duplicates are adjacent, so we can detect runs of identical elements. We need to identify runs of length exactly 2. We iterate through the array, and whenever we find two consecutive equal elements, we check if this run is exactly two by looking at the elements before and after the pair. Specifically, for a pair at indices i and i+1: if (i == 0 or arr[i-1] != arr[i]) and (i+2 >= n or arr[i+2] != arr[i]), then it's exactly two occurrences. The first condition checks that the element before is different (or we're at the start), and the second checks that the element after is different (or we're at the end). This ensures we don't count runs of 3 or more. The algorithm uses a while loop to traverse the array, skipping ahead appropriately when we find a pair. Time complexity is O(n) for the scan, and space is O(1) excluding output. This feeds into L0 by providing the duplicate detection step after sorting. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in index checks, not handling array boundaries correctly, or misidentifying runs of more than two. The real code pattern involves a loop with if conditions for equality and boundary checks.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize i=0, result=[]. Step 2: While i < n-1: i=0, arr[0]=1, arr[1]=2, not equal, i++ to 1. i=1, arr[1]=2, arr[2]=2, equal, check exactly two: (i==0? false, so check arr[0]=1 !=2 true) and (i+2=3<n, arr[3]=3 !=2 true), add 2 to result, i+=2 to 3. i=3, arr[3]=3, arr[4]=3, equal, check: (i>0, arr[2]=2 !=3 true) but (i+2=5<n, arr[5]=3 ==3) false (next is also 3, so run of three), skip, i++ to 4. i=4, arr[4]=3, arr[5]=3, equal, check: (i>0, arr[3]=3 ==3) false (prev is also 3), skip, i++ to 5. i=5, arr[5]=3, arr[6]=4, not equal, i++ to 6. i=6, i+1=7>=n, stop. Result: [2].

Step 1: i=0, result=[]. i=0, arr[0]=5, arr[1]=5, equal, check: (i==0 true) and (i+2=2<n, arr[2]=6 !=5 true), add 5 to result, i+=2 to 2. i=2, arr[2]=6, arr[3]=6, equal, check: (i>0, arr[1]=5 !=6 true) but (i+2=4<n, arr[4]=6 ==6) false (run of three), skip, i++ to 3. i=3, arr[3]=6, arr[4]=6, equal, check: (i>0, arr[2]=6 ==6) false, skip, i++ to 4. i=4, arr[4]=6, arr[5]=7, not equal, i++ to 5. i=5, i+1=6>=n, stop. Result: [5].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identify Consecutive Equal Elements in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Scan a sorted array to find pairs of adjacent duplicate elements.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental insight: after sorting, duplicates become neighbors, so we can detect them by simply comparing each element with the next one. The intuition is that sorting groups identical values together, reducing the problem from searching across the entire array to a local comparison. Without sorting, finding duplicates would require extra data structures like hash maps for efficient lookup, but sorting allows an in-place, space-efficient solution. The algorithm iterates through the array from index 0 to n-2. For each index i, if arr[i] == arr[i+1], then we have found a duplicate pair. We don't yet worry about counting exactly two occurrences; that's handled in L1. Here, we just identify any consecutive duplicates. Time complexity is O(n) for the linear scan, and space is O(1). This feeds into L1 by providing the basic duplicate detection, which is then refined to check for exactly two. Common mistakes include going out of bounds by comparing arr[i] with arr[i+1] when i is the last index, or not skipping ahead after finding a pair (which can lead to double-counting in runs of more than two). The real code pattern is a for-loop or while-loop with a condition to check equality of adjacent elements.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize list of pairs. Step 2: For i from 0 to 5 (n-2=5): i=0, arr[0]=1, arr[1]=2, not equal. i=1, arr[1]=2, arr[2]=2, equal, add pair (1,2). i=2, arr[2]=2, arr[3]=3, not equal. i=3, arr[3]=3, arr[4]=4, not equal. i=4, arr[4]=4, arr[5]=4, equal, add pair (4,5). i=5, arr[5]=4, arr[6]=4, equal, add pair (5,6). Result: pairs at (1,2), (4,5), (5,6).

Step 1: Initialize pairs. Step 2: For i from 0 to 4: i=0, arr[0]=5, arr[1]=5, equal, add (0,1). i=1, arr[1]=5, arr[2]=6, not equal. i=2, arr[2]=6, arr[3]=7, not equal. i=3, arr[3]=7, arr[4]=7, equal, add (3,4). i=4, arr[4]=7, arr[5]=8, not equal. Result: pairs at (0,1), (3,4).

ladders.title: Building Block — Sort an Array of Integers
ladders.desc: Sort an array of integers in non-decreasing order using an efficient algorithm.
ladders.explanation: Sorting is a prerequisite for the sort-and-scan approach. The intuition is that arranging elements in order groups duplicates together, making them easy to find with a simple scan. We use an efficient comparison-based sorting algorithm like quicksort, mergesort, or heapsort, which have average or worst-case time complexity of O(n log n). For simplicity, we'll assume using a built-in sort function, but the concept applies to any O(n log n) sort. The algorithm takes an unsorted array and rearranges its elements so that each element is less than or equal to the next element. This ordering property ensures that any duplicate values become adjacent. Time complexity is O(n log n) for efficient sorts, and space complexity can be O(1) for in-place sorts like quicksort or O(n) for mergesort. This feeds into L2 by providing the sorted array needed for duplicate detection. Common mistakes include using inefficient sorts like bubble sort (O(n^2)), not handling edge cases like empty arrays, or misunderstanding stable vs. unstable sorts. The real code pattern is calling a sort function, e.g., arr.sort() in Python, which uses Timsort (a hybrid sort).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with [4, 2, 7, 1, 3]. Step 2: Apply a sorting algorithm (e.g., quicksort): Choose pivot, partition, and recursively sort. For simplicity, trace of built-in sort: After sort, elements are rearranged: compare and swap as needed. Final order: 1, 2, 3, 4, 7.

Step 1: Unsorted: [5, 5, 3, 3, 1]. Step 2: Sort using an efficient method: For example, mergesort divides into subarrays, merges in order. After sorting, duplicates are grouped: 1, then two 3's, then two 5's. Result: [1, 3, 3, 5, 5].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Adjacent Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array and compare each element with its next neighbor.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation used in the scan step. The intuition is that by comparing each element with the one next to it, we can detect local relationships like duplicates or order. In the context of finding duplicates after sorting, this comparison reveals when two consecutive elements are equal. The algorithm involves looping through the array from the first index to the second-to-last index. For each index i, we access arr[i] and arr[i+1] and check if they are equal. This requires careful index management to avoid going out of bounds. Time complexity is O(n) for a single pass, and space is O(1). This feeds into L3 and L2 by providing the mechanism to identify consecutive duplicates after sorting. Common mistakes include accessing arr[i+1] when i is the last index (causing an index error), or not initializing the loop correctly. The real code pattern is a for-loop with range up to len(arr)-1, or a while-loop with a condition on i < n-1.

examples.output: Comparisons: (1,2) not equal, (2,2) equal, (2,3) not equal, (3,4) not equal
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize i=0. Step 2: While i < n-1 (4): i=0, compare arr[0]=1 and arr[1]=2, not equal. i=1, compare arr[1]=2 and arr[2]=2, equal. i=2, compare arr[2]=2 and arr[3]=3, not equal. i=3, compare arr[3]=3 and arr[4]=4, not equal. i=4, i not < n-1, stop.
Step 1: i=0, compare arr[0]=5 and arr[1]=5, equal. i=1, compare arr[1]=5 and arr[2]=6, not equal. i=2, compare arr[2]=6 and arr[3]=6, equal. i=3, i not < n-1, stop.

testCases.expected: Comparisons: (1,2) not equal, (2,2) equal, (2,3) not equal, (3,4) not equal
Comparisons: (2,4) not equal, (4,6) not equal, (6,8) not equal
Comparisons: (1,1) equal, (1,2) not equal, (2,2) equal, (2,3) not equal

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Access Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Understand how to access individual elements in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: knowing how to work with arrays and indices. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index (usually starting from 0). The intuition is that by using indices, we can retrieve or compare specific elements, which is essential for scanning and sorting. For example, to compare adjacent elements, we need to access arr[i] and arr[i+1]. The algorithm involves declaring an array and using square brackets with an integer index to get the value at that position. Time complexity for access is O(1), and space is O(n) for the array storage. This feeds into L4 by enabling the comparisons between elements. Common mistakes include using indices out of bounds (e.g., negative or >= array length), confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based, or not initializing the array properly. The real code pattern is defining an array like arr = [1, 2, 3] and accessing arr[0] to get 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array arr = [10, 20, 30]. Step 2: Access arr[0]: index 0 refers to the first element, value is 10. Step 3: Access arr[1]: index 1 refers to the second element, value is 20. Step 4: Access arr[2]: index 2 refers to the third element, value is 30. Step 5: Trying arr[3] would be out of bounds (error).
Step 1: arr = [5, 5, 6]. Step 2: arr[0] gives the first element: 5. Step 3: arr[1] gives the second element: 5. Step 4: These can be compared for equality.

approaches.name: In-Place Marking O(n)
approaches.summary: Leverage the constraint that array elements are within 1 to n. Use the array indices as markers by negating values to indicate visited elements, identifying duplicates when encountering a negative value.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Duplicates Using In-Place Marking
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find all elements appearing exactly twice in an array using index-based marking.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires identifying all elements that appear exactly twice in an array of size n, where elements are in the range [1, n]. The in-place marking approach leverages the constraint that array values correspond to valid indices (after adjusting for 0-based indexing). The intuition is that we can use the array itself as a marker system: when we encounter a value, we look at the position it points to (value-1) and mark it as visited by negating it. If we ever try to mark a position that's already negative, we know we've seen this value before, so it's a duplicate. This works because: 1) All values are positive initially, 2) Each value maps to a unique index, 3) Negation preserves the original value's magnitude while indicating visitation. The algorithm iterates through each element, calculates the target index as abs(nums[i])-1, checks if nums[target] is negative (indicating a duplicate), otherwise negates nums[target] to mark it as visited. We use abs() to handle values that might have been negated by previous operations. Time complexity is O(n) since we process each element once. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables and modify the input array in-place. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete marking and duplicate collection logic. Common mistakes include: not using abs() when accessing values (leading to index errors), forgetting to handle 0-based indexing correctly, or modifying the array while iterating in ways that skip elements. The key insight is transforming the array into a self-referential data structure where values serve as both data and pointers.

examples.trace:
Step 1: i=0, val=4, idx=3, nums[3]=7 (positive) → negate to -7, array: [4,3,2,-7,8,2,3,1]
Step 2: i=1, val=3, idx=2, nums[2]=2 (positive) → negate to -2, array: [4,3,-2,-7,8,2,3,1]
Step 3: i=2, val=abs(-2)=2, idx=1, nums[1]=3 (positive) → negate to -3, array: [4,-3,-2,-7,8,2,3,1]
Step 4: i=3, val=abs(-7)=7, idx=6, nums[6]=3 (positive) → negate to -3, array: [4,-3,-2,-7,8,2,-3,1]
Step 5: i=4, val=8, idx=7, nums[7]=1 (positive) → negate to -1, array: [4,-3,-2,-7,8,2,-3,-1]
Step 6: i=5, val=2, idx=1, nums[1]=-3 (negative) → duplicate found: 2, array unchanged
Step 7: i=6, val=abs(-3)=3, idx=2, nums[2]=-2 (negative) → duplicate found: 3, array unchanged
Step 8: i=7, val=abs(-1)=1, idx=0, nums[0]=4 (positive) → negate to -4, array: [-4,-3,-2,-7,8,2,-3,-1]
Result: Duplicates collected during steps 6 and 7: [2,3]

Step 1: i=0, val=1, idx=0, nums[0]=1 (positive) → negate to -1, array: [-1,1,2]
Step 2: i=1, val=1, idx=0, nums[0]=-1 (negative) → duplicate found: 1, array unchanged
Step 3: i=2, val=2, idx=1, nums[1]=1 (positive) → negate to -1, array: [-1,-1,2]
Result: Duplicate collected at step 2: [1]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Mark Visited Elements and Detect Duplicates
ladders.desc: The core marking logic that processes one element to mark its corresponding index or identify duplicates.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the essential operation of the in-place marking approach: for a given array value, determine whether it has been seen before by checking the marker at its corresponding index. The intuition is that each value v in the array (where 1 ≤ v ≤ n) corresponds to index v-1 in 0-based indexing. We use this index as a marker position. If the value at that marker position is positive, we haven't seen v before, so we mark it by negating the value at the marker. If it's already negative, we have seen v before, so it's a duplicate. The algorithm takes the current index i, gets the value val = abs(nums[i]) (using abs() because previous markings may have negated values), computes target index idx = val-1, checks if nums[idx] < 0. If true, val is a duplicate. If false, we mark by setting nums[idx] = -nums[idx]. Time complexity is O(1) per element. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 as the repeated application of this sub-routine across all elements solves the full problem. Common mistakes include: using nums[i] directly without abs() (which fails if the value was negated by marking another element), incorrect index calculation (forgetting -1), or not preserving the original value when marking (negation preserves magnitude). The key insight is that negation creates a binary state (positive=unvisited, negative=visited) while keeping the original value recoverable via abs().

examples.trace:
Step 1: Get val = abs(nums[5]) = abs(2) = 2
Step 2: Compute idx = val-1 = 2-1 = 1
Step 3: Check nums[1] = 3 (positive from earlier steps? Actually after previous markings, let's assume array is [4,-3,-2,-7,8,2,-3,-1] at this point)
Wait, let's trace properly from start: Initial array: [4,3,2,7,8,2,3,1]
After processing i=0 to 4: array becomes [4,-3,-2,-7,8,2,-3,-1] (as in L0 example)
Now i=5: val=abs(2)=2, idx=1, nums[1]=-3 (negative) → duplicate detected: 2

Step 1: val = abs(nums[0]) = abs(1) = 1
Step 2: idx = 1-1 = 0
Step 3: nums[0] = 1 (positive) → mark by setting nums[0] = -1
Result: Array becomes [-1,1,2], no duplicate detected

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using Array Indices as Markers via Negation
ladders.desc: The central technique of representing visited states by negating values at specific indices.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core insight of the approach: using the array indices themselves as markers to track which values have been seen. The intuition stems from the constraint that array elements are in the range [1, n], meaning each value v can be mapped to a unique index v-1 (converting to 0-based). Since all values are positive initially, we can use the sign bit as a marker: positive means unvisited, negative means visited. When we encounter a value v, we look at index v-1. If the value there is positive, we negate it to mark v as visited. If it's already negative, v has been visited before, indicating a duplicate. This works because: 1) The mapping v → v-1 is bijective for values 1..n, 2) Negation doesn't change the magnitude, so we can recover the original value with abs(), 3) We process elements sequentially, so markings accumulate. The algorithm doesn't require extra space because we repurpose the input array. Time complexity for marking one element is O(1). Space is O(1). This connects to L1 as this logic is encapsulated in the sub-routine. Common mistakes include: not handling the 1-to-n range assumption, trying to mark values outside this range, or misunderstanding that we're marking the index corresponding to the value, not the current position. The key insight is that the array serves as both data storage and a visitation map through strategic negation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Value v = 4
Step 2: Map to index: idx = v-1 = 4-1 = 3
Step 3: Check array[3] = 7 (positive)
Step 4: Negate array[3] to -7
Result: Index 3 is now marked as visited (negative), indicating value 4 has been seen

examples.input: Value 2 in array after some markings: [4,-3,-2,-7,8,2,-3,-1]
examples.trace:
Step 1: Value v = 2 (using abs() if needed, but here it's positive)
Step 2: Map to index: idx = 2-1 = 1
Step 3: Check array[1] = -3 (negative)
Step 4: Since it's negative, value 2 has been visited before → duplicate
Result: Duplicate 2 detected without modifying array

ladders.title: Building Block — Mapping Values to Indices in 1-to-n Range
ladders.desc: Converting array values to valid array indices using the constraint that elements are between 1 and n.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves leveraging the problem constraint that all array elements are in the range [1, n], where n is the array size. This allows us to safely use each value v as a pointer to index v-1 in the array (adjusting for 0-based indexing). The intuition is that because v is between 1 and n inclusive, v-1 is between 0 and n-1 inclusive, which are valid indices for an array of size n. This mapping is crucial because it lets us use the array itself as a lookup table: instead of needing a separate data structure to track visited elements, we can store the visitation state directly at the index corresponding to each value. The algorithm is simple: given a value v, compute idx = v-1. This requires the array to be 0-indexed. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L2 as this mapping enables the index-based marking technique. Common mistakes include: forgetting to subtract 1 (leading to index-out-of-bounds), assuming 1-based indexing, or not verifying the 1-to-n constraint holds. The key insight is that the value range restriction transforms the array into a self-indexing structure where values can directly reference positions within the same array.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Value v = 4
Step 2: Array size n = 8, so valid indices are 0 to 7
Step 3: Compute idx = v-1 = 4-1 = 3
Step 4: Check: 3 is between 0 and 7 → valid index
Result: Value 4 maps to index 3

Step 1: Value v = 1
Step 2: Array size n = 5, valid indices 0-4
Step 3: Compute idx = 1-1 = 0
Step 4: Check: 0 is between 0 and 4 → valid index
Result: Value 1 maps to index 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Negating Values to Represent Binary States
ladders.desc: Using sign changes to mark elements as visited/unvisited while preserving original values.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves negating a number to change its sign while keeping its magnitude intact. In the context of the algorithm, we use negation to represent a binary state: positive means unvisited, negative means visited. The intuition is that we need a way to mark that a particular value has been seen without losing the original value information, because we might need to reference it later (e.g., when it appears as a value pointing to another index). Negation achieves this: if original value is v, after negation it becomes -v. The magnitude |v| remains the same, so we can recover v using absolute value (abs()). This is efficient because sign change is an O(1) operation and doesn't require extra memory. The algorithm simply takes a number x and returns -x. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L3 as negation is applied at the indices mapped from values. Common mistakes include: negating zero (which stays zero, but not an issue here since values are 1..n), or not considering that negation might overflow in some languages (but with 1..n range, it's safe). The key insight is that sign provides a free bit of information that we can repurpose as a visitation flag without altering the data needed for further computations.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Original value = 7
Step 2: Apply negation: -7
Step 3: Magnitude remains 7, sign changed from positive to negative
Result: -7 represents 'visited' state for value 7

Step 1: Value = -3 (already marked as visited)
Step 2: To recover original value, take absolute value: abs(-3) = 3
Step 3: This allows us to use the original value 3 for index mapping
Result: 3 is the original value, recovered from negated form

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Value Ranges
ladders.desc: Understanding how array indices work and the meaning of value constraints like 1 ≤ x ≤ n.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers two key ideas: 1) Array indexing in programming typically starts at 0, so an array of size n has indices 0, 1, 2, ..., n-1. 2) When we say values are in the range [1, n], it means each value is an integer between 1 and n inclusive. The intuition is that these two facts together allow us to connect values to indices: if we subtract 1 from any value v in [1, n], we get a number between 0 and n-1, which is a valid index. For example, if n=5, values can be 1,2,3,4,5. Subtracting 1 gives 0,1,2,3,4, which are exactly the indices of a 5-element array. This is important because it means every value in the array 'points to' a unique position within the same array. There's no time or space complexity here—it's a basic property. This connects to L4 as it enables the mapping used in negation marking. Common mistakes include: confusing 0-based vs 1-based indexing, misunderstanding inclusive ranges, or not seeing the relationship between values and indices. The key insight is that the value constraint creates a perfect mapping from array elements to array positions, which is the bedrock of the entire in-place marking approach.

examples.input: Array size n=4, values must be in [1,4]
examples.trace:
Step 1: Array has 4 elements, so indices are 0, 1, 2, 3
Step 2: Values are between 1 and 4 inclusive: 1, 2, 3, 4
Step 3: Subtract 1 from each value: 1-1=0, 2-1=1, 3-1=2, 4-1=3
Step 4: These results (0,1,2,3) match the array indices
Result: Every value corresponds to a unique index

Step 1: Value v = 3
Step 2: Array size n = 5, so valid indices are 0,1,2,3,4
Step 3: Check: 3 is between 1 and 5? Yes
Step 4: Compute index = v-1 = 3-1 = 2
Step 5: Check: 2 is between 0 and 4? Yes
Result: Value 3 maps to index 2
